caveats: a: BLS combines ship and boat building rather than measuring 336611 alone.
a: Occupation shares are not task shares and do not distinguish already automated CAD, CNC, and planning work.
rho: NIST covers manufacturing generally, not shipyards.
rho: Navy evidence describes industrial constraints rather than directly measuring AI implementation.
e: No source measures the exact share of LMM firms meeting all scope, independence, repeat-revenue, and transferability tests.
e: Government and commercial shipyards have materially different customer and contract structures.
s5: Gallup covers employer businesses across industries rather than shipyards or the target EBITDA band.
s5: Stated intentions do not equal completed qualifying control changes.
q: This is discounted retained gross benefit rather than accounting margin.
q: Navy repair contracting may be more transparent and competitive than commercial or emergency work, while major new construction may be more concentrated.
d5: GAO evidence is Navy-heavy and does not cover all commercial shipyard demand.
d5: Maintenance backlog is unmet work, not guaranteed funded revenue, and the most recent Navy plan rests on assumptions GAO found unrealistic.
o: This measures persistence of the accountable shipyard role, not survival of any specific yard.
o: Some repair quantity can migrate outside 336611 without the underlying maintenance need disappearing.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Recurring or repeat outsourced revenue from shipowners and government agencies through maintenance availabilities, repair, overhaul, conversion, modernization, emergency work, and repeat new-build programs.
lens.excluded_activities: Boat building outside shipyards, floating-drydock repairs classified as water-transport support, component fabrication or trade subcontracting classified by production process, captive government yards, and firms without repeat external-customer work.
lens.included_activities: U.S. lower-middle-market shipyards with fixed facilities and fabrication equipment that build, repair, convert, alter, dismantle, or scale commercial, government, and other nonrecreational ships, barges, workboats, and floating platforms for external customers.

a.bridge: Source population/quantity: May 2023 BLS employment for NAICS 336600, where production occupations were 46.83%, architecture and engineering 9.70%, and installation, maintenance, and repair 6.21%. Transformation: assigned plausible currently exposed task fractions within these occupations and supporting office functions, discounting manual, access-constrained, safety-critical, and accountable work. Error direction: inclusion of smaller recreational boat plants may overstate repeatable fabrication exposure, while shipyard design, planning, documentation, and digital-quality work may make the estimate conservative.
caveats: BLS combines ship and boat building rather than measuring 336611 alone.
Occupation shares are not task shares and do not distinguish already automated CAD, CNC, and planning work.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted task inventory across independent shipyards, separating new construction from repair and already automated work from incremental AI exposure, would materially move the range.
a.rationale: Exposure is concentrated in design reuse, estimating, planning, scheduling, procurement, documentation, inspection assistance, nesting and cutting optimization, inventory, and some robotic fabrication. Drydock work, access-limited repair, welding, pipefitting, rigging, electrical installation, testing, and accountable engineering remain highly physical and variable.

d5.bridge: Source population/quantity: GAO reports a $2.3 billion Navy surface-maintenance backlog, a planned fleet increase from 296 ships in September 2024 toward 381, but also roughly one-third less projected private repair work for fiscal 2027 than previously expected and unstable construction plans. Transformation: combined backlog, fleet and modernization direction with forecast volatility into a five-year constant-price, constant-quality demand interval for U.S. LMM shipyards. Error direction: Navy evidence can overstate total-industry growth if funds and schedules slip, but excludes commercial, offshore, inland, Coast Guard, and emergency demand that could make the central case conservative.
caveats: GAO evidence is Navy-heavy and does not cover all commercial shipyard demand.
Maintenance backlog is unmet work, not guaranteed funded revenue, and the most recent Navy plan rests on assumptions GAO found unrealistic.
d5.change_evidence: A reconciled five-year schedule of Navy, Coast Guard, MARAD, Jones Act, inland-barge, offshore-energy, and commercial repair and new-build spending converted to constant-quality labor and dock demand would materially refine the ratio.
d5.rationale: An aging fleet, deferred maintenance, national-security goals, vessel modernization, and constrained yard capacity support durable work. Demand remains lumpy: Navy plans have been unstable, decommissioning can remove repair volume, commercial shipping cycles shift projects, and large contracts can move between years or yards.

caveats: No source measures the exact share of LMM firms meeting all scope, independence, repeat-revenue, and transferability tests.
Government and commercial shipyards have materially different customer and contract structures.
e.change_evidence: A MARAD shipyard census linked to ownership, Master Ship Repair Agreement status, customer mix, repeat-revenue history, and normalized EBITDA would directly measure eligibility.
e.rationale: Independent yards performing repeat commercial or government repair, modernization, conversion, and vessel programs fit the lens. Eligibility falls for captive public yards, project-only new-build firms lacking repeat customers, component and trade subcontractors outside the code, distressed yards without transferable qualifications, and businesses dominated by recreational boats or floating-drydock support.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 336611 ($8,955,341,408) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 336611 ($30,842,615,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.0904 (margins.json[336]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Auto Parts EBITDA/Sales 9.04%); revenue/employee $314,096 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 336611); class <5: 88 firms, avg revenue/firm $515,477 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $46,599 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 51 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,397,549 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $126,338 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 62 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,759,984 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $339,903 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 119 firms, avg revenue/firm $10,961,227 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $990,895 -> out of $1-10M band; class 100-499: 53 firms, avg revenue/firm $56,771,906 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $5,132,180 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 37 firms, avg revenue/firm $707,555,730 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $63,963,038 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 53

caveats: This measures persistence of the accountable shipyard role, not survival of any specific yard.
Some repair quantity can migrate outside 336611 without the underlying maintenance need disappearing.
o.change_evidence: Fleet-owner procurement data quantifying work shifted to self-maintenance, foreign yards, floating-drydock providers, mobile repair teams, or vessel retirement would materially move the surviving operator-required share.
o.rationale: Ships remain large physical assets requiring drydocks, heavy fabrication, skilled trades, testing, certification, environmental control, and an accountable yard. Digital design, autonomous inspection, robotics, and remote diagnostics change execution but do not eliminate the operator; displacement is limited to owner self-maintenance, foreign yards, afloat repair outside the code, or vessel retirement.

caveats: This is discounted retained gross benefit rather than accounting margin.
Navy repair contracting may be more transparent and competitive than commercial or emergency work, while major new construction may be more concentrated.
q.change_evidence: Contract-level data from LMM yards on fixed-price, cost-type, and time-and-material work; bid margins; change orders; audit clauses; recompete cadence; and post-improvement pricing would materially move retention.
q.rationale: Facility scarcity, drydock access, workforce qualifications, customer approval, emergency responsiveness, and local fleet proximity can preserve some implemented benefit. Retention is reduced by competitive government procurement, detailed cost scrutiny, fixed-price risk, change-order negotiation, multi-year rebids, commercial buyer power, and customers' ability to defer or move work.

rho.bridge: Source population/quantity: NIST reports 46% of manufacturers using AI tools and more than 80% expecting increased use within two years, while GAO documents shipyard workforce and infrastructure constraints. Transformation: converted general adoption into the share of shipyard-exposed tasks expected to be operational within five years, discounted for capital, cybersecurity, data, qualification, and low-volume workflow barriers. Error direction: stated adoption plans may overstate realized deployment, while acute schedule and skilled-labor pressure could accelerate proven tools beyond the base case.
caveats: NIST covers manufacturing generally, not shipyards.
Navy evidence describes industrial constraints rather than directly measuring AI implementation.
rho.change_evidence: MARAD Small Shipyard Grant outcomes or Navy shipyard digital-transformation records reporting production deployments, labor hours avoided, schedule effects, and adoption by yard size would materially refine implementation.
rho.rationale: Five-year implementation should be meaningful in digital planning, computer vision, documentation, supply-chain analytics, design assistance, predictive maintenance, and selected robotics. Legacy yards, fragmented data, cyber and defense requirements, union and workflow change, low-volume projects, capital intensity, quality assurance, and physical constraints slow deployment.

s5.bridge: Source population/quantity: Gallup found 22% of U.S. employer-business owners planned to sell or transfer within five years and 52.3% of employer businesses had owners age 55 or older. Transformation: used the intention rate as the central anchor and widened for execution risk and specialized industrial succession pressure. Error direction: intentions likely overstate completed qualifying transfers, while unplanned health, distress, or strategic sales offset part of the conversion shortfall.
caveats: Gallup covers employer businesses across industries rather than shipyards or the target EBITDA band.
Stated intentions do not equal completed qualifying control changes.
s5.change_evidence: Five years of shipyard transactions from PitchBook, GF Data, Axial, and maritime brokers, combined with owner-age and succession interviews among MARAD small-yard grant recipients, would materially refine the probability.
s5.rationale: Broad employer-owner succession evidence indicates a material five-year transfer pool, but shipyard transactions require environmental, facility, customer-qualification, bonding, workforce, and contract diligence. Plans may become closures, minority financings, family continuity, or nonqualifying transfers.

principal_driver: Aging, mission-critical fleets require recurring physical repair and modernization at qualified facilities with scarce drydock and trade capacity.
principal_weakness: Large, irregular contracts and unstable government workload signals can leave expensive facilities and skilled workforces underutilized.

sources.supports: Exact shipyard scope, included building and repair activities, and exclusions for component trades and floating-drydock repair.
sources.title: 2022 NAICS Manual

sources.supports: Occupational structure used to bridge to wage-weighted task exposure.

sources.supports: Manufacturing AI adoption, use cases, expected expansion, and implementation barriers.
sources.title: The Rise of Artificial Intelligence (AI) in U.S. Manufacturing

sources.supports: Employer-business owner age and five-year sale or transfer intentions.
sources.title: Most Small-Business Owners Lack a Succession Plan

sources.supports: Private-yard structure, repair qualifications, backlog, workforce and capacity constraints, competition, grants, forecast volatility, and projected fleet and repair workload.
sources.title: Shipbuilding and Repair: Navy Needs a Strategic Approach for Private Sector Industrial Base Investments

sources.supports: Current assessment that the latest Navy shipbuilding plan relies on historically unsupported backlog, cost, and schedule assumptions.
